Summary: This study investigates the utility of conventional and advanced imaging techniques, including radiomics and deep learning, in differentiating pseudoprogression (PsPD) from true progressive disease (PD) in glioblastoma patients post-chemoradiotherapy. The study found that currently available imaging biomarkers, including ADC, nrCBV, and radiomic features, did not reliably differentiate PsPD from PD and did not improve models built with clinical data and MGMT methylation status. The authors conclude that larger, multi-institutional studies are needed to build more reliable models.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of the contrast-enhancing (CE) and T2-FLAIR components of the tumor using a deep learning-based tool (HD-GLIO).

Condition2.reason: The study utilizes a fully automated deep learning-based segmentation tool (HD-GLIO) for tumor segmentation, without human intervention.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features from the segmented tumor regions, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data, including clinical data, MGMT methylation status, median ADC and nrCBV values, and radiomic features, in their machine learning models.

Condition5.reason: The study includes end-to-end deep learning models using ResNet architectures to classify PsPD vs. PD directly from image data.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study defines clear inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming for a representative study population. Inclusion criteria are explicitly defined in the Methods section.

Item3.reason: The reference standard (true PD vs. PsPD) is defined based on follow-up MRI scans according to RANO criteria, which is a reasonable approach given the lack of histological confirmation for all patients. The definition of PD and PsPD is clearly stated.

Item4.reason: The study involves two institutions as diagnostic imaging data sources: the Department of Neuroradiology of the Heidelberg University Hospital and the Department of Radiology of the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ).

Item5.reason: The imaging data source (MRI) is clinically translatable, as MRI is a standard imaging technique for glioblastoma follow-up. The study uses standard MRI sequences and parameters.

Item6.reason: The study provides details on MRI acquisition parameters in the Supplementary Materials, ensuring replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that the evaluated time period was set to 7 months after the end of RT, based on a previous study. The classification of patients into either true PD or PsPD was set through the evaluation of follow-up MRI scans, as previously reported. True PD was defined as a further lesion increase at 1 of the 2 subsequent follow-up MRI scans (performed at least 4 weeks after the MRI of initial suspicion) or as a tumor-related fatal outcome within 6 months after the initial enhancement increase. Patients were classified as PsPD if the initial enhancement increase was stable or reduced within the 2 subsequent follow-up MRI scans and in absence of death at 6 months.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of the HD-GLIO-AUTO pipeline and HD-GLIO tool for automated DL-based segmentation of the CE and T2-FLAIR components of the tumor.

Item9.reason: The study does not include a formal evaluation of the fully automated segmentation accuracy compared to domain expert annotations (e.g., DICE score or Jaccard index).

Item10.reason: The study uses a fully automated tool (HD-GLIO) for segmentation, which means the test set segmentation masks are produced by an automated tool without human intervention.

Item11.reason: The study describes the image preprocessing techniques used, including brain extraction, registration, N4 bias field correction, and intensity normalization. The study also mentions gray level discretization parameters (binwidth of 25) for radiomic feature extraction.

Item12.reason: The study uses PyRadiomics for feature extraction, which is a standardized software. The study mentions the use of PyRadiomics (https://pyradiomics.readthedocs.io/).

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomic features) and provides information on the feature extraction parameters, including the bin width. The radiomic features extracted are listed in the Supplementary Materials.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of non-robust features through test-retest, reproducibility analysis, or stability analysis.

Item15.reason: The study uses LASSO (least absolute shrinkage and selection operator) as an embedded feature-selection method within the generalized linear models, which reduces dimensionality by selecting more informative features.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification or assessment of the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size. There is no statistical justification for the number of features used in relation to the sample size.

Item17.reason: The study does not include a robustness assessment of the end-to-end deep learning pipelines in a test-retest setting or through stability analysis.

Item18.reason: The study uses a 5-times repeated 5-fold crossvalidation resampling procedure. To mitigate the problem of class imbalance, as patients with PsPD were less than those with early PD, a synthetic minority oversampling technique was used.

Item19.reason: The study addresses potential confounding factors by implementing the ComBat feature harmonization technique to counteract possible batch effects in radiomics features due to MRI acquisition on different scanners.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, sensitivity, and specificity for the classification task. Confidence intervals are also provided.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures by reporting 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity).

Item22.reason: The study does not include a calibration assessment of the final models.

Item23.reason: The study does not include a comparison of uni-parametric imaging with multi-parametric imaging to justify the need for the multi-parametric approach. The study uses multiple MRI sequences without justifying their combined use compared to individual sequences.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of models including radiomic features with a model based on clinical data and MGMT promoter methylation status, which can be considered a non-radiomic approach representative of clinical practice. DeLong's test is used to compare AUCs.

Item25.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the models with a simple baseline reference model (such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier) to justify the use of machine learning methods.

Item26.reason: The model is tested using cross-validation, which can be considered internal testing on data from the same source as the training data.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any imaging, segmentation, clinical, or radiomics analysis data being shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study mentions the use of HD-GLIO-AUTO pipeline, but it is unclear whether all scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling are shared with the public with clear instructions for their implementation.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the availability of the final model in any form (raw model file or ready-to-use system) for public use.
